entities.description: Matthew's Early English Text Society edition-level compilation of twenty-eight numbered Middle English tracts, editorial arguments and notes, an appendix, and a glossarial index.
entities.label: Frederic David Matthew, The English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted (1880)

physicalDescription.extent: li preliminary pages, one unnumbered leaf, 572 Arabic-numbered pages, and a frontispiece

next_steps: Inspect copy-specific cataloging or colophon evidence beyond the preserved 1880 title matter; retain the reprint and facsimile relations as qualified meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: Is the Internet Archive source copy demonstrably a specific Kraus 1975 reprint, and what new reprint matter, if any, does it contain?

next_steps: Inspect MS 74 and modern tract handlists; do not create a tract-XX Sidney relation without new evidence.
openQuestions.question: Why does Matthew's front manuscript list assign Sidney Sussex A.4.12 to no. XX while the tract XII argument explicitly assigns its Ave Maria collation there and tract XX says it comes only from CCCC MS 296?

next_steps: Retry the public viewer manually or ask TCD Digital Collections; current reCAPTCHA blocked automated audit, and the exact DataCite rightsList is empty.
openQuestions.question: What exact canvas inventory, physical coverage, tract loci, and item-level image license apply to the TCD MS 244 Digital Collections object?

next_steps: Use the recorded institutional enquiry routes and audit any supplied inventories before creating facsimile entities or completeness claims.
openQuestions.question: Can complete public reproductions and exact tract loci be established for TCD MS 245, Bodley 647, Sidney Sussex MS 74, New College MS 95, and Lambeth MS 551?

next_steps: Seek repository and U-M clarification; meanwhile keep the repository artifact status unknown and record both dated U-M statements without merging them.
openQuestions.question: Does the pinned CME Unlicense expressly apply to inherited XML/data, and how should the legacy U-M copyright-may-apply notice be reconciled with the current item-specific NoC-US statement?

relations.subjectLabel: Frederic David Matthew, The English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted (1880)

reviewNotes: Matthew's umbrella Wyclif attribution is historical edition metadata, not a present authorship conclusion for all twenty-eight tracts.
Every manuscript-source relation is tract- or passage-scoped. Complete facsimile coverage is qualified relative to the provider's exposed inventory.
Matthew's printed Corpus shelfmark '290' is preserved as historical wording/error; the exact current codex and content match CCCC MS 296.
The explicit Sidney Sussex tract-XII collation statement governs the graph; the contradictory front-list 'No. XX' remains unresolved.
Physical manuscript ownership, textual public-domain status, editorial copyright, catalog metadata, page-image licensing, and access terms are not conflated.

lineage.summary: aeh6713 is the repository XML for a 2006 University of Michigan uncorrected OCR/Level-1 TEI encoding derived from Frederic David Matthew's 1880 edition of twenty-eight numbered Middle English Wycliffite tracts. The XML verbally encodes title/printer matter and tracts on pp. 1–482 while linking most preliminaries and pp. 483–572 only as page images. Matthew's edition draws base text from nine identified codices and collates five of them in additional tract-scoped roles; no manuscript is represented as containing the whole twenty-eight-tract compilation. Complete exposed IIIF inventories are cached for CCCC MS 296 and John Rylands English MS 86, the Bodley 788 cache is explicitly selected and target-absent, and TCD MS 244 is retained as metadata-only because bot mitigation prevented a reliable canvas audit. Rights are separated across medieval objects, 1880 edition, provider-described 1975 reprint, U-M encoding and page images, repository artifact, catalogs, and modern facsimiles.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous and disputed Wycliffite writers; traditionally attributed to John Wyclif
catalogSummary.date: Late fourteenth to early fifteenth century; Matthew edition published 1880
catalogSummary.title: The English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted

catalogingSubject.label: The English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The edition-level electronic and editorial compilation represented by University of Michigan CME item AEH6713.0001.001: Frederic David Matthew's 1880 Early English Text Society volume of twenty-eight numbered Middle English tracts historically attributed to John Wyclif. The record keeps the twenty-eight medieval tract children, their disputed and multiple authorships, Matthew's editorial matter, the physical manuscripts, the 1880 printed edition, later reprint carrier, U-M encoding, linked but untranscribed page images, and repository XML distinct. It does not describe Wyclif's complete English oeuvre or imply that any one witness contains the whole compilation.

contentParts.label: Encoded title and printer matter
contentParts.summary: The 1880 title page and printer statement are verbally encoded.

contentParts.label: Page-linked preliminary matter not transcribed in the XML

locators.value: frontispiece and preliminary matter through p. li, excluding the separately encoded title page and printer verso

notes: Presence as linked PB/EPB milestones is not treated as verbal transcription.
contentParts.summary: The frontispiece and the remaining preliminary leaves through p. li are represented by linked page-image milestones but contain no OCR-transcribed verbal text in the XML.

contentParts.label: Twenty-eight numbered medieval tracts
contentParts.summary: The edition's twenty-eight numbered Middle English religious, devotional, ecclesiological, and polemical tracts, each preceded by Matthew's editorial argument where supplied.

contentParts.label: I. Of the Leaven of Pharisees
contentParts.summary: A polemic against hypocrisy and the religious orders, organized in eleven chapters; its editorial argument includes a two-line Chaucer quotation.

contentParts.label: II. How Men Ought to Obey Prelates
contentParts.summary: On the limits of obedience to prelates and the priority of divine law.

contentParts.label: III. The Rule and Testament of St. Francis
contentParts.summary: A vernacular treatment of the Franciscan Rule and Testament directed against later departures from Franciscan poverty.

contentParts.label: IV. Of Prelates
contentParts.summary: An extended polemic on clerical office, wealth, authority, and reform.

contentParts.label: V. Speculum de Antichristo
contentParts.summary: A short mirror of Antichrist focused on corrupt clerical and religious practice.

contentParts.label: VI. Of Clerks Possessioners
contentParts.summary: A forty-chapter argument against endowed and property-holding clergy.

contentParts.label: VII. How the Office of Curates Is Ordained of God
contentParts.summary: A thirty-three-chapter account of pastoral office, preaching, and clerical responsibility.

contentParts.label: VIII. The Order of Priesthood
contentParts.summary: A twenty-nine-chapter treatment of priestly office and discipline.

contentParts.label: IX. Three Things Destroy This World
contentParts.summary: A short tract identifying destructive abuses in ecclesiastical and social life.

contentParts.label: X. Of Feigned Contemplative Life
contentParts.summary: A polemic against counterfeit contemplative religion; Matthew restores a passage displaced within CCCC MS 296.

contentParts.label: XI. The Paternoster
contentParts.summary: A vernacular exposition of the Lord's Prayer.

contentParts.label: XII. The Ave Maria
contentParts.summary: A vernacular exposition of the Ave Maria.

contentParts.label: XIII. How Satan and His Children Turn Works of Mercy Upside Down and Deceive Men Therein and in Their Five Wits
notes: Matthew's contents assigns the opening to p. 210, while the tract division and page milestones begin at p. 209.
contentParts.summary: A three-chapter treatment of works of mercy and the five senses.

contentParts.label: XIV. How Religious Men Should Keep Certain Articles
contentParts.summary: Instruction and polemic concerning the obligations of members of religious orders.

contentParts.label: XV. Of Servants and Lords
contentParts.summary: A social and religious treatment of reciprocal duties between servants and lords.

contentParts.label: XVI. Why Poor Priests Have No Benefice
contentParts.summary: A three-chapter defense of poor priests and critique of the benefice system.

contentParts.label: XVII. How Antichrist and His Clerks Travail to Destroy Holy Writ
notes: Matthew's contents prints p. 253, while the tract division and page milestones begin at p. 254.
contentParts.summary: A five-chapter defense of scripture against ecclesiastical obstruction.

contentParts.label: XVIII. How Satan and His Priests and His Feigned Religious Cast by Three Cursed Heresies to Destroy All Good Living and Maintain All Manner of Sin
contentParts.summary: A four-chapter polemic against three alleged heresies used to sustain clerical abuse.

contentParts.label: XIX. Of Poor Preaching Priests
contentParts.summary: A short defense of itinerant poor preachers.

contentParts.label: XX. Augustinus (translation fragment)
contentParts.summary: A brief Middle English translation fragment attributed in the edition heading to Augustine.

contentParts.label: XXI. Of Dominion
contentParts.summary: A four-chapter tract on lordship and dominion.

contentParts.label: XXII. Tractatus de Pseudo-Freris
contentParts.summary: An eight-chapter polemic against false friars.

contentParts.label: XXIII. Of Confession
contentParts.summary: A tract on confession, clerical mediation, and penitential practice.

contentParts.label: XXIV. Of Faith, Hope and Charity
contentParts.summary: A six-chapter exposition of the three theological virtues.

contentParts.label: XXV. De Sacramento Altaris
contentParts.summary: A short tract on the sacrament of the altar.

contentParts.label: XXVI. The Clergy May Not Hold Property
notes: The appendix on pp. 393–404 is internal to numbered tract XXVI and is not counted as a twenty-ninth medieval work.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous radical Wycliffite tract against clerical property, followed on pp. 393–404 by Matthew's internally titled appendix on secular office and authorities.

contentParts.label: XXVII. De Officio Pastorali
contentParts.summary: A thirty-two-chapter treatise on pastoral office, preaching, tithes, and clerical poverty.

contentParts.label: XXVIII. De Papa
contentParts.summary: A twelve-chapter polemic on the pope and papal office.

contentParts.label: Editorial appendix on papal citations
notes: The pages are linked as images; their words are absent from the XML text stream.
contentParts.summary: Extracts from two Latin tracts concerning references to popes, printed in Matthew's appendix but not verbally transcribed in the XML.

contentParts.label: Editorial notes
contentParts.summary: Matthew's back-of-volume notes, linked by page images but not verbally transcribed in the XML.

contentParts.label: Glossarial index
contentParts.summary: The edition's glossarial index, linked by page images but not verbally transcribed in the XML.

notes: This is the publication date of the edition-level compilation, not a medieval composition date.

dateStatements.display: Matthew dates tract I to about 1383 from its reference to Bishop Despenser's crusade
notes: The normalized year records Matthew's historical argument and awaits modern tract-specific review.

dateStatements.display: Tract XXVI predates March 1413 and is probably early fifteenth century
notes: Hudson says the same anonymous scholar had already treated clerical temporalities in this sermon before composing a different tract between March 1413 and February 1414; the evidence does not date tract XXVI itself to 1413–1414.

extent.description: Twenty-eight numbered prose tracts on printed pp. 1–482. Within the tracts the XML contains 28 DIV1, 262 DIV2, 4 DIV3, 322 HEAD, 499 P, 483 PB, 436 MILESTONE, 1,381 NOTE1, and 27 TABLE elements. The only L elements are two lines inside one Q1 quotation in tract I's editorial argument. Across the file there are 31 DIV1: two empty front-matter page wrappers, twenty-eight tracts, and one empty back-matter wrapper; 638 PB and 639 EPB milestones link the larger scanned book.

formStatements.description: Prose compilation. Tract I's editorial argument quotes one two-line verse passage; the quotation does not make the medieval tract or compilation mixed-form.

scope.description: Prayer expositions and instruction on faith, hope, and charity
Tracts on clerical, pastoral, ecclesial, and papal office
Modern English appears in title matter, Matthew's arguments, summaries, notes, and reference matter
Latin appears in tract titles, epigraphs, authorities, formulas, and quoted passages

notes: The title and inherited U-M author field are historical attribution evidence, not proof that Wyclif authored all twenty-eight tracts.
The record distinguishes medieval composition, manuscript production, Matthew's 1880 edition, a provider-described later reprint carrier, U-M's 2006 OCR/TEI encoding, and the repository artifact.
Element totals are representation metrics from the XML, not critical-text extents.
A witness used only for collation is bound as a related source and is not silently promoted to base witness.

next_steps: Review tract-level bibliographies and modern editions, preserving Matthew's opinions as attribution history rather than present consensus.
openQuestions.question: What are the current specialist attribution and composition-date assessments for each of the twenty-eight tracts?

next_steps: Inspect MS 74 and modern handlists to test whether the front-list 'No. XX' is a typographical error; retain only the explicit tract-XII collation relation meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: Why does Matthew's front manuscript list associate Sidney Sussex A.4.12 with no. XX when the tract XII argument explicitly says that the Ave Maria was collated with that manuscript and tract XX says it comes only from CCCC MS 296?

next_steps: Request item-level production notes from U-M while preserving the directly observed encoded-versus-image-linked boundary.
openQuestions.question: What production decision caused the U-M XML to link but not OCR-transcribe most front matter and all back matter on printed pp. 483–572?

notes: The statement does not transfer the production place of a particular surviving manuscript to every tract.
London is the title-page place of publication, not the composition place of the medieval tracts.
Matthew selected, ordered, introduced, and annotated the twenty-eight numbered tracts and supplied the back matter.

The title and U-M discovery field preserve the historical attribution. Current Parker cataloging treats the principal manuscript collection as multi-author, and this record does not transfer the umbrella attribution into twenty-eight secure authorships.

scope.description: The twenty-eight numbered medieval tracts under the edition's umbrella attribution

notes: This collective responsibility neither claims that every tract is anonymous nor rules out Wyclif's authorship of an individual tract.

scope.description: Collective description of the multi-author Wycliffite tract collection; individual assignments remain unresolved

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous radical Wycliffite scholar of the early fifteenth century
notes: Hudson identifies Matthew pp. 362–404 with an earlier sermon by the anonymous scholar; the more securely dated Tractatus de Oblacione Iugis Sacrificii is a later work by that author, not the same text.

subjects.label: Church reform, clerical authority, and religious orders

scope.description: Tracts addressing clerical possessions, benefices, dominion, secular office, and tithes
Prayer expositions and tracts on scripture, confession, virtues, and the Eucharist

subjects.label: Authorship, attribution, and the construction of the Wycliffite corpus

summaries.value: Matthew's 1880 Early English Text Society volume gathers twenty-eight numbered Middle English tracts concerned with religious orders, clerical authority and poverty, scripture, prayer, confession, sacraments, dominion, pastoral office, and the papacy. The title preserves a historical Wyclif attribution, but the principal CCCC MS 296 collection is now cataloged as multi-author and individual tract attributions remain uneven. Tract XXVI can be associated with the anonymous radical Wycliffite scholar studied by Anne Hudson, without importing the 1413–1414 date of that scholar's later work.

The XML verbally encodes the title and printer matter plus the twenty-eight numbered tracts on printed pp. 1–482, including Matthew's tract arguments and inline notes. Other preliminary matter and the back appendix, notes, and glossarial index on pp. 483–572 survive in the digital object as linked PB/EPB page-image milestones but have no OCR-transcribed words in the XML. The three empty page-wrapper paragraphs are not counted as content.

work.summary: Matthew's 1880 Early English Text Society volume gathers twenty-eight numbered Middle English tracts concerned with religious orders, clerical authority and poverty, scripture, prayer, confession, sacraments, dominion, pastoral office, and the papacy. The title preserves a historical Wyclif attribution, but the principal CCCC MS 296 collection is now cataloged as multi-author and individual tract attributions remain uneven. Tract XXVI can be associated with the anonymous radical Wycliffite scholar studied by Anne Hudson, without importing the 1413–1414 date of that scholar's later work.
